msDesc.head: Gospels (Codex Oxoniensis, sometimes called the Gospels of St Augustine); Italy, late 6th or 7th century

msItem.title: Gospels
Matthew
msItem.note: Begins incomplete at Matthew 4.14; a leaf missing after fol. 6 containing Matthew 8.29 - 9.18.

Manuscript 'O' in the edition by Wordsworth and White (Oxford, 1898); manuscript Jo in the collations by Bonifatius Fischer (Freiburg, 1988-1991). The text, which belongs to the Italian family, is discussed by Marsden, who comments that the relationship between our manuscripts and Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, MS. 286 (the other 'Augustine Gospels') "has often been overstressed" (pp. 289-292, at p. 289). Marsden discusses the capitula briefly at p. 292.
Matthew copied continuously, the other gospels per cola et commata
Extensive corrections to Matthew and the first two chapters of Mark; word-division added to Matthew.

Ganz distinguishes the following annotations:
Annotations by two Italian scribes identifying lections for feast days, printed Ganz, p. 38 (scribe 1, the main scribe of the volume), and pp. 38-9 (scribe 2)
Corrections and annotations by an English scribe writing both insular minuscule (e.g. fols. 23r, 26v, 27r, 32v, 35r, 36v) and uncial (fols. 74r, 164v) (Ganz, p. 39)
Liturgical texts added by another scribe in insular minuscule (fols. 34r, 149v, the latter referring to St Chad) (Ganz, pp. 40-1)
Annotations in uncial and insular minuscule, concentrated in Matthew, again identifying lections (Ganz, pp. 41-43); and added punctuation.
Longer notes added in the late 11th or early 12th century (fols. 3v, from Ambrose, De sacramentis, V.4, fol. 135v, and fol. 158r, referring to a reading 'ad collationem sicut consuetudines docent'); this hand altered the chapter titles to Mark and Luke and probably made other corrections (Ganz, pp. 43-44).
One Old English gloss, fol. 19vb, 'on haele'. Not in Ker; noted by Doane.

supportDesc.support: parchment (arranged according to Gregory's rule, flesh side outside)
supportDesc.extent:
iii + 172 + 1 + ii leaves, foliated i-iii, 1-176 (fol. 173 is an endleaf perhaps originally from another manuscript: see below) c. 245 c. 200
Cropped with loss of marginalia.
supportDesc.collation: [One quire missing] 2(8-2), 1st and 8th leaves missing (fols. 1-6); 3-22(8) (fols. 7-166); 23(8-2), 7th and 8th leaves missing (fols. 167-172), [one quire missing], one endleaf (fol. 173). Quire-signatures: 'qiii' (f. 14v), 'qiiii' (f. 22v), etc., ending 'qxxii' (f. 166v).

layoutDesc.layout: 2 col. of 29 lines, written space c. 210 c. 160 Ruled in drypoint 'apparently each bifolium separately' (Lowe) with single vertical bounding lines on fols. 1-38 and double thereafter.

handDesc.handNote: Uncial script by one main scribe.

musicNotation.p: Anglo-French neums and noted versicles (11th cent.) added to the Genealogy of Christ (Luke, fols. 79r-80r); Anglo-Saxon neums, fol. 158v: see Ganz (p. 37 and n. 11) and Hartzell for further details.

decoDesc.summary: Pächt and Alexander ii. 1a: 'Initial' (referring to fol. 40v?)

history.provenance: Lowe attributed the manuscript to southern Italy in the 7th century (CLA II(2).230), and has largely been followed by later scholarship (e.g Barker-Benfield in Brown, ed., cat. 53: Italy (?), late sixth or seventh century).
In England by late 8th century, as shown by annotations.

The medieval provenance of the manuscript is uncertain. Its identification as one of the gospel books sent by Gregory I to Augustine of Canterbury was apparently first suggested by Humphrey Wanley (Parkes, p. 74). Barker-Benfield thought that a connection with St Augustine's Canterbury was 'not entirely to be ruled out', as this manuscript (together with Corpus Cambridge MS. 286) is 'the only other extant gospel book from around Gregory's time or not much later to meet the criterion of having been imported early into England' (III.1734-5). The 8th-century annotation on fol. 149v suggests a connection with Lichfield. The 11th or 12th-century annotation on fol. 158r referring to a passage read at 'collation' shows that the manuscript was then in a monastic house. The current fol. 173 (for which see below) was not necessarily part of the volume in the middle ages - it may have been added by Cotton - and therefore does not provide clear evidence that the manuscript was at Bury St Edmunds.
Owned by Sir Robert Cotton in 1602.
history.acquisition: Given by him to the Library in 1603.

recordHist.source: Summary description by Matthew Holford, 2026, with reference to published literature as cited. Previously described in the Summary Catalogue. Summary Catalogue, Vol. 2, part 1, p. 500 Summary Catalogue, Vol. 2, part 1, p. 501 Summary Catalogue, Vol. 2, part 1, p. 502

availability.p: To ensure its preservation, access to this item is restricted, and readers are asked to work from reproductions and published descriptions as far as possible. If you wish to apply to see the original, please click the request button above. When your request is received, you will be asked to contact the relevant curator outlining the subject of your research, the importance of this item to that research, and the resources you have already consulted.

surrogates.bibl: A. N. Doane, Anglo-Saxon Manuscripts in Microfiche Facsimile (Binghamton, NY: Arizona Center for Medieval and Renaissance Studies, 2002), vol. 7: Anglo-Saxon Bibles and the 'Book of Cerne'
Microfilm available in the open shelf collections in the Weston Library (R. Films 101)

listBibl.head: Selected bibliography:
listBibl.bibl: Parkes, M. B., ‘History in Books’ Clothing: Books as Evidence for Cultural Relations between England and the Continent in the Seventh and Eighth Centuries’, in Pages from the Past: Medieval Writing Skills and Manuscript Books, ed. by P. R. Robinson and Rivkah Zim (Ashgate, 2012)

Brown, Michelle P. (ed.), In the Beginning: Bibles before the Year 1000 [Exhib. Cat.][Washington, D.C., Freer Gallery of Art & Arthur M. Sackler Gallery, Smithsonian Institution, 21 Oct. 2006-7 Jan. 2007] (Smithsonian, Freer Gallery of Art & Arthur M. Sackler Gallery, 2006), cat. 53 [by Bruce Barker-Benfield]
Barker-Benfield, B. C. (ed.), St Augustine’s Abbey, Canterbury, Corpus of British Medieval Library Catalogues 13 (The British Library in association with The British Academy, 2008), III, 1734-5
Hartzell, K. D., Catalogue of Manuscripts Written or Owned in England up to 1200 Containing Music (Boydell Press in association with the Plainsong and Medieval Music Society, 2006), no. 242
A. N. Doane, in Anglo-Saxon Manuscripts in Microfiche Facsimile (Binghamton, NY: Arizona Center for Medieval and Renaissance Studies, 2002), vol. 7: Anglo-Saxon Bibles and the 'Book of Cerne'

Ganz, David, ‘The Annotations in Oxford, Bodleian Library, Auct. D. II. 14’, in Belief and Culture in the Middle Ages: Studies Presented to Henry Mayr-Harting, ed. by Richard Gameson and Henrietta Leyser (Oxford University Press, 2001)
Marsden, Richard, ‘The Gospels of St Augustine’, in St. Augustine and the Conversion of England, ed. by Richard Gameon (1999), pp. 285–312
Otto Pächt and J. J. G. Alexander, Illuminated Manuscripts in the Bodleian Library, Oxford, II (1970), no. 1a
N. R. Ker, Catalogue of Manuscripts containing Anglo-Saxon (Oxford, 1957), no. 290 (on fol. 173)
S. J. P. Van Dijk, Handlist of the Latin Liturgical Manuscripts in the Bodleian Library Oxford : Vol. 1: Mass Books (typescript, 1957), p. 64
Codices Latini Antiquiores II, 2nd ed., no. 230

listBibl.head: Online resources:
listBibl.bibl: The Production and Use of English Manuscripts 1060 to 1220 [on fol. 173]

msItem.title: Booklist and list of names
msItem.note: Most recently edited: English Benedictine Libraries: The Shorter Catalogues, CBMLC 4 (1996), B12.

msItem.title: Ad introitum porte
msItem.note: [ps] Fundamenta eius in montibus [v] Diliget dominus. Dominus vobiscum. [oratio] Domine Ihesu Christe qui introitum portarum. Said after the lustration of holy water.

history.provenance: Bury St Edmunds, Benedictine abbey: 'Bealdewuine abb.', fol. 173r: the only abbot Baldwin in England before the middle of the 12th century was Baldwin, abbot of Bury St Edmunds (d. 1098).

revisionDesc.change: Matthew Holford: description revised for publication on Digital Bodleian.
James Cummings Up-converted the markup using https://github.com/jamescummings/Bodleian-msDesc-ODD/blob/master/convertTolkien2Bodley.xsl
